project_name: mysql to bigquery data ingestion pipeline
objective: Design and implement a reliable and scalable MySQL to BigQuery pipeline to support analytics, reporting, and long-term data storage, including a one-time full historical load for all identified tables and ongoing scheduled incremental loads (hourly or daily per table) thereafter.
business_context: The analytics team requires reliable access to MySQL source data in Google BigQuery to support reporting and long-term data storage. All agreed-upon tables must be present in BigQuery with complete historical data and reliable scheduled data refreshes so the analytics team has full confidence in the data. Stakeholder-sponsored initiative owned by Shruthi B, currently in draft pending stakeholder review.
functional_requirements: Perform a one-time full historical load from MySQL to BigQuery for all identified tables without data loss
Build and deploy a scheduled incremental data load pipeline from MySQL to BigQuery, running hourly or daily depending on per-table configuration
Use timestamp-based or primary key-based change tracking to identify and load only new or changed records each incremental cycle
Gate incremental loads so they cannot begin until the full historical load has been validated and confirmed complete
Implement pre-load schema validation that detects type mismatches, null handling inconsistencies, and field mapping issues between MySQL and BigQuery schemas
Define and implement a strategy for handling new columns that appear in the MySQL source (e.g., alert and hold, or auto-map with review)
Implement per-load data quality checks covering row count reconciliation, primary key uniqueness, and data type constraint verification
Maintain audit logs for all load executions capturing execution time, record counts, validation results, and any errors
Provide stakeholder visibility into load success through accessible logs or a dashboard without requiring engineering involvement

data_types: structured relational data with timestamp and/or primary key columns
data_requirements.frequency: daily schedule interval; hourly or daily per-table configuration for incremental loads

non_functional.scalability: Pipeline must be reliable and scalable to support analytics, reporting, and long-term data storage
non_functional.sla: Historical load must be 100% complete without data loss; scheduled hourly or daily incremental jobs must run without failures on the defined cadence

constraints.technical_limitations: Incremental loads must not begin until historical load validation has passed; change tracking must use timestamps or primary keys — full table re-scans are not permitted

inferred_assumptions: Cloud environment is GCP given the use of Google BigQuery as the target system
Technology stack likely includes GCP-native or compatible orchestration and ingestion tooling, though no specific frameworks or services are named in the requirements
Source tables contain timestamp and/or primary key columns based on the change-tracking requirements described for incremental loads
acceptance_criteria: All agreed-upon tables are present and complete in BigQuery
Historical load is 100% complete without data loss
Scheduled hourly or daily jobs run without failures on defined cadence
Analytics team confirms data is reliable for reporting
Row counts are validated between MySQL and BigQuery for historical loads
Primary key uniqueness is verified in BigQuery after each load
Null handling and data type constraints are validated before load completes
Audit logs are maintained for every load execution
Incremental loads do not begin until historical load validation passes
Incremental jobs execute on defined hourly or daily schedule without failures
Change tracking correctly captures all new and modified records each cycle
No duplicate or missed records occur between consecutive load cycles
Change tracking uses timestamps or primary keys — full table re-scans are not permitted
Each incremental run is logged with execution time, record counts, and status
All schema mismatches are detected and logged before each load proceeds
Row counts match between MySQL and BigQuery after every load execution
Primary key uniqueness and data type constraints are verified per load run
Audit logs are complete and accessible for every load execution
Stakeholders can review load success independently via logs or dashboard without engineering involvement
Schema validation executes before every load — mismatches are logged and flagged
New MySQL columns are handled according to a defined strategy (e.g., alert and hold, or auto-map with review)
Row counts reconcile between MySQL source and BigQuery target within an accepted threshold
Audit logs capture load timestamp, row counts ingested, validation results, and any errors
expected_outputs: All agreed-upon MySQL tables present in Google BigQuery with complete historical data
Reliable scheduled incremental data refresh pipeline running hourly or daily per table configuration
Pre-load schema validation reports detecting type mismatches, null handling inconsistencies, and field mapping issues
Per-load data quality check results covering row count reconciliation, primary key uniqueness, and data type constraint verification
Audit logs for every load execution capturing execution time, record counts, validation results, and errors
Stakeholder-accessible logs or dashboard enabling independent load success verification without engineering involvement

Description:
project name: mysql to bigquery data ingestion pipeline requirement type: new_dvlp stakeholder: shruthi b schedule interval: daily objective design and implement a reliable and scalable mysql to bigquery pipeline to support analytics, reporting, and long-term data storage design and implement a reliable and scalable pipeline that loads identified mysql tables into google bigquery on a scheduled basis (hourly or daily depending on the table) to support analytics, reporting, and long-term data storage. requires both a one-time full historical load for all identified tables and ongoing scheduled incremental loads thereafter. item currently in draft pending stakeholder review. expected outcome all agreed-upon tables present in bigquery with complete historical data and reliable scheduled data refresh; analytics team has full confidence in the data ##connections and db details mysql to bq acceptance criteria success conditions: all agreed-upon tables are present and complete in bigquery historical load is 100% complete without data loss scheduled hourly or daily jobs run without failures on defined cadence analytics team confirms data is reliable for reporting validation rules: row counts must be validated between mysql and bigquery for historical loads primary key uniqueness must be verified in bigquery after each load null handling and data type constraints must be validated before load completes audit logs must be maintained for every load execution incremental loads must not begin until historical load validation passes

Comments:
[2026-04-27T08:47:40.580-0500] Ahemad ALi Shaik: 🤖 *Requirements Agent Summary*

Request Type: *new development*
Ingestion Type: *batch*  |  Output Type: *pipeline*  (confidence 78%)
Run Plan: data_model → architecture → implementation_steps

Objective: Design and implement a reliable and scalable MySQL to BigQuery pipeline to support analytics, reporting, and long-term data storage, including a one-time full historical load for all identified tables and ongoing scheduled incremental loads (hourly or daily per table) thereafter.

Business Context:
The analytics team requires reliable access to MySQL source data in Google BigQuery to support reporting and long-term data storage. All agreed-upon tables must be present in BigQuery with complete historical data and reliable scheduled data refreshes so the analytics team has full confidence in the data. Stakeholder-sponsored initiative owned by Shruthi B, currently in draft pending stakeholder review.

Functional Requirements:
Perform a one-time full historical load from MySQL to BigQuery for all identified tables without data loss
Build and deploy a scheduled incremental data load pipeline from MySQL to BigQuery, running hourly or daily depending on per-table configuration
Use timestamp-based or primary key-based change tracking to identify and load only new or changed records each incremental cycle
Gate incremental loads so they cannot begin until the full historical load has been validated and confirmed complete
Implement pre-load schema validation that detects type mismatches, null handling inconsistencies, and field mapping issues between MySQL and BigQuery schemas
Define and implement a strategy for handling new columns that appear in the MySQL source (e.g., alert and hold, or auto-map with review)
Implement per-load data quality checks covering row count reconciliation, primary key uniqueness, and data type constraint verification
Maintain audit logs for all load executions capturing execution time, record counts, validation results, and any errors
Provide stakeholder visibility into load success through accessible logs or a dashboard without requiring engineering involvement

Acceptance Criteria:
All agreed-upon tables are present and complete in BigQuery
Historical load is 100% complete without data loss
Scheduled hourly or daily jobs run without failures on defined cadence
Analytics team confirms data is reliable for reporting
Row counts are validated between MySQL and BigQuery for historical loads
Primary key uniqueness is verified in BigQuery after each load
Null handling and data type constraints are validated before load completes
Audit logs are maintained for every load execution
Incremental loads do not begin until historical load validation passes
Incremental jobs execute on defined hourly or daily schedule without failures
Change tracking correctly captures all new and modified records each cycle
No duplicate or missed records occur between consecutive load cycles
Change tracking uses timestamps or primary keys — full table re-scans are not permitted
Each incremental run is logged with execution time, record counts, and status
All schema mismatches are detected and logged before each load proceeds
Row counts match between MySQL and BigQuery after every load execution
Primary key uniqueness and data type constraints are verified per load run
Audit logs are complete and accessible for every load execution
Stakeholders can review load success independently via logs or dashboard without engineering involvement
Schema validation executes before every load — mismatches are logged and flagged
New MySQL columns are handled according to a defined strategy (e.g., alert and hold, or auto-map with review)
Row counts reconcile between MySQL source and BigQuery target within an accepted threshold
Audit logs capture load timestamp, row counts ingested, validation results, and any errors
